painPoints: Status updates that ramble without structure
Clients asking 'so what's the bottom line?'
Taking too long to explain project progress
Not knowing what details to include or exclude

conversion.nextStep: Book a free strategy call to master confident project communication

seo.metaDescription: Get the 60-second status update formula that executives love. Stop rambling in meetings and start communicating like a senior leader.

en.title: The 60-Second Status Update Script
en.subtitle: The Formula That Executives Actually Want to Hear
en.description: Stop rambling through status updates. This proven formula lets you communicate project progress in 60 seconds—with the structure senior leaders expect.
en.headline: Give Status Updates That Get Respect, Not Glazed-Over Eyes
en.valueProposition: Learn the exact 4-part formula that makes executives lean in instead of tuning out.

leadCapture.successMessage: Check your inbox! Your script is on its way.

sections.title: Why Most Status Updates Fail
sections.content:
Here's what executives hear when you give a typical status update: 'blah blah blah technical detail blah blah blah we're working on it blah blah blah.'

They're not listening because you're not speaking their language. They want three things: Where are we? What's the risk? What do you need? Everything else is noise.

This 4-part formula gives them exactly that—in 60 seconds or less.

items.title: S = Status (10 seconds)
items.content: Lead with the headline. Are you on track, at risk, or blocked? Don't bury the lead in details—executives want the bottom line first.

example.wrong: So we've been working on the integration and there have been some challenges with the API documentation and we're trying to work through them...
example.right: Project X is on track for the March 15 delivery.
example.why: The executive now knows the most important thing. Everything else is context.

items.actionItem: Start every status update with ONE sentence that answers: 'Are we on track?'

items.title: T = Timeline (15 seconds)
items.content: Give the key milestones—what's done, what's next, and when. Use dates, not vague timeframes.

example.wrong: We finished the first phase recently and we're working on the next part now. It should be done soon.
example.right: Phase 1 completed last Friday. Phase 2 starts Monday and delivers March 1. Final delivery March 15.
example.why: Specific dates show you have control. Vague timelines signal uncertainty.

items.actionItem: Always have three dates ready: last milestone, next milestone, final delivery.

items.title: A = At Risk (20 seconds)
items.content: Proactively flag anything that could derail the project. If nothing's at risk, say so clearly. If something is, name it and your mitigation plan.

example.wrong: Well, there are a few things we're keeping an eye on... the vendor might be late and there's a resource question...
example.right: One risk: the vendor API documentation is incomplete. Mitigation: I've scheduled a call with their team Thursday to resolve.
example.why: Executives respect people who see problems coming. They lose trust in people who get surprised.

items.actionItem: Before every status meeting, ask yourself: 'What could go wrong this week?'

items.title: R = Request (15 seconds)
items.content: End with what you need—a decision, resource, or just acknowledgment. If you need nothing, say that too.

example.wrong: So yeah, that's where we are... any questions? I guess we'll just keep going...
example.right: I need one thing from you: approval to bring in a contractor for two weeks. That keeps us on schedule.
example.why: Clear requests get clear answers. Vague endings get forgotten.

items.actionItem: End every status update with 'I need...' or 'No blockers—we're good to continue.'

sections.title: The Complete 60-Second Script
sections.content:
Here's how it sounds when you put it all together:

'Project X is on track for the March 15 delivery. [STATUS]

Phase 1 completed last Friday. Phase 2 starts Monday and delivers March 1. Final delivery March 15. [TIMELINE]

One risk: the vendor API documentation is incomplete. Mitigation: I've scheduled a call with their team Thursday to resolve. [AT RISK]

I need one thing from you: approval to bring in a contractor for two weeks. That keeps us on schedule. [REQUEST]'

Total time: 45 seconds. Total confidence: 100%.

es.title: El script de actualización de estado en 60 segundos
es.subtitle: La fórmula que los ejecutivos realmente quieren escuchar
es.description: Deja de divagar en actualizaciones de estado. Esta fórmula probada te permite comunicar el progreso del proyecto en 60 segundos—con la estructura que los líderes senior esperan.
es.headline: Da actualizaciones de estado que generan respeto, no miradas perdidas
es.valueProposition: Aprende la fórmula exacta de 4 partes que hace que los ejecutivos presten atención en lugar de desconectarse.

leadCapture.formSubheadline: La fórmula de 60 segundos que los ejecutivos aman
leadCapture.successMessage: ¡Revisa tu bandeja! Tu script está en camino.

sections.title: Por qué la mayoría de las actualizaciones de estado fallan
sections.content:
Esto es lo que los ejecutivos escuchan cuando das una actualización típica: 'bla bla bla detalle técnico bla bla bla estamos trabajando en eso bla bla bla.'

No están escuchando porque no hablas su idioma. Quieren tres cosas: ¿Dónde estamos? ¿Cuál es el riesgo? ¿Qué necesitas? Todo lo demás es ruido.

Esta fórmula de 4 partes les da exactamente eso—en 60 segundos o menos.

items.title: S = Status/Estado (10 segundos)
items.content: Empieza con el titular. ¿Estamos en camino, en riesgo o bloqueados? No entierres lo importante en detalles—los ejecutivos quieren la conclusión primero.

example.wrong: So we've been working on the integration and there have been some challenges with the API documentation and we're trying to work through them...
example.right: Project X is on track for the March 15 delivery.
example.why: El ejecutivo ya sabe lo más importante. Lo demás es contexto.

items.actionItem: Empieza cada actualización con UNA oración que responda: '¿Estamos en camino?'

items.title: T = Timeline/Cronograma (15 segundos)
items.content: Da los hitos clave—qué está hecho, qué sigue y cuándo. Usa fechas, no plazos vagos.

example.wrong: We finished the first phase recently and we're working on the next part now. It should be done soon.
example.right: Phase 1 completed last Friday. Phase 2 starts Monday and delivers March 1. Final delivery March 15.
example.why: Las fechas específicas muestran control. Los plazos vagos generan desconfianza.

items.actionItem: Ten siempre tres fechas listas: último hito, próximo hito y entrega final.

items.title: A = At Risk/En riesgo (20 segundos)
items.content: Identifica proactivamente cualquier cosa que pueda descarrilar el proyecto. Si nada está en riesgo, dilo claramente. Si algo sí lo está, nómbralo junto a tu plan de mitigación.

example.wrong: Well, there are a few things we're keeping an eye on... the vendor might be late and there's a resource question...
example.right: Un riesgo: la documentación del API del proveedor está incompleta. Mitigación: ya programé una llamada con su equipo el jueves para resolverlo.
example.why: Los ejecutivos confían en quien anticipa problemas, no en quien se sorprende por ellos.

items.actionItem: Antes de cada reunión pregúntate: '¿Qué podría salir mal esta semana?'

items.title: R = Request/Solicitud (15 segundos)
items.content: Termina con lo que necesitas—una decisión, un recurso o simplemente confirmación. Si no necesitas nada, dilo también.

example.wrong: So yeah, that's where we are... any questions? I guess we'll just keep going...
example.right: Necesito una cosa de tu parte: aprobación para contratar a un consultor durante dos semanas. Eso nos mantiene en el cronograma.
example.why: Solicitudes claras obtienen respuestas claras. Finales vagos se olvidan.

items.actionItem: Termina cada actualización con 'Necesito...' o 'Sin bloqueos—podemos continuar.'

sections.title: El script completo de 60 segundos
sections.content:
Así suena cuando lo unes todo:

'Project X is on track for the March 15 delivery. [STATUS]

Phase 1 completed last Friday. Phase 2 starts Monday and delivers March 1. Final delivery March 15. [TIMELINE]

One risk: the vendor API documentation is incomplete. Mitigation: I've scheduled a call with their team Thursday to resolve. [AT RISK]

I need one thing from you: approval to bring in a contractor for two weeks. That keeps us on schedule. [REQUEST]'

Tiempo total: 45 segundos. Confianza total: 100%.
